title: Public holidays

meta.description: Employee entitlements on public holidays — right to be absent, payment, working on public holidays, and substitution.

headings.text: Right to be absent on public holidays
Reasonable request to work on a public holiday

content:
Employees have a right to be absent from work on a public holiday. The NES (s.114 Fair Work Act) gives employees this right.

Right to be absent:
An employee is entitled to be absent from work on a public holiday. An employer may request an employee to work on a public holiday, but the request must be reasonable. An employee may refuse a request that is unreasonable.

Factors in determining whether a request or refusal is reasonable:
- The nature of the employer's business and the employee's role
- The employee's personal circumstances (including family responsibilities)
- Whether the employee is being compensated for working on the public holiday
- The amount of notice given
- Whether the employee has religious or cultural beliefs that make the day significant

An employer cannot deliberately change an employee's roster to avoid paying public holiday entitlements.

Pay on public holidays:
- If an employee doesn't work on a public holiday that falls on a day they would ordinarily work, they must be paid their ordinary pay for that day
- If an employee works on a public holiday, they must be paid at least the public holiday penalty rate set by their award or registered agreement (often 225%–250% of ordinary rate)
- If an employee is not ordinarily required to work on that day (e.g. a part-time employee not rostered that day), they have no entitlement to payment

Casual employees:
- A casual employee is entitled to public holiday pay only if they would have ordinarily been rostered to work on that day
- Casual employees who work on a public holiday are paid the applicable award penalty rate (which includes their casual loading)

Substituting public holidays:
- By agreement between an employer and employee, a different day can be substituted for a public holiday
- Some awards also allow this substitution by agreement
- State or Territory legislation may also substitute public holidays (e.g. if a public holiday falls on a weekend, a substitute day may be declared)

Which public holiday applies:
An employee is entitled to the public holidays that apply in the location where they are based for work — not where they are physically working on the day. This is important for employees who travel for work.

National public holidays (apply to all states and territories):
- New Year's Day (1 January)
- Australia Day (26 January)
- Good Friday
- Easter Saturday
- Easter Sunday
- Easter Monday
- Anzac Day (25 April)
- Queen's Birthday / King's Birthday (date varies by state)
- Christmas Day (25 December)
- Boxing Day (26 December)

Additional public holidays vary by state and territory (e.g. Melbourne Cup Day in Victoria, Royal Queensland Show Day in Brisbane/QLD).
tables: Employee ordinarily works that day — doesn't work
Paid ordinary rate for the day

key_facts.part_time_entitlement: Only on days they are ordinarily rostered to work
key_facts.casual_entitlement: Only if ordinarily rostered for that day
